title: निर्वाण षट्कम् (आत्म षट्कम्)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री निर्वाण षट्कम् ॥

मनो बुद्ध्यहंकार चित्तानि नाहं
न च श्रोत्रजिह्वे न च घ्राणनेत्रे।
न च व्योम भूमिर्न तेजो न वायुः
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥१॥

न च प्राणसंज्ञो न वै पञ्चवायुः
न वा सप्तधातुर्न वा पञ्चकोषः।
न वाक्पाणिपादं न चोपस्थपायु
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥२॥

न मे द्वेषरागौ न मे लोभमोहौ
मदो नैव मे नैव मात्सर्यभावः।
न धर्मो न चार्थो न कामो न मोक्षः
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥३॥

न पुण्यं न पापं न सौख्यं न दुःखं
न मन्त्रो न तीर्थं न वेदा न यज्ञाः।
अहं भोजनं नैव भोज्यं न भोक्ता
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥४॥

न मृत्युर्न शंका न मे जातिभेदः
पिता नैव मे नैव माता न जन्म।
न बन्धुर्न मित्रं गुरुर्नैव शिष्यः
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥५॥

अहं निर्विकल्पो निराकाररूपो
विभुत्वाच सर्वत्र सर्वेन्द्रियाणाम्।
न चासंगतं नैव मुक्तिर्न मेयः
चिदानन्दरूपः शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहम्॥६॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यविरचितं निर्वाणषट्कं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Nirvana Shatkam ||

Mano Buddhyahankara Chittani Naham
Na Cha Shrotrajihve Na Cha Ghrananeetre.
Na Cha Vyoma Bhoomirna Tejo Na Vayuh
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||1||

Na Cha Pranasanjno Na Vai Panchavayuh
Na Vaa Saptadhaaturna Vaa Panchakoshah.
Na Vaakpaanipaadam Na Chopasthapaayu
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||2||

Na Me Dvesharaagau Na Me Lobhamohau
Mado Naiva Me Naiva Maatsaryabhaavah.
Na Dharmo Na Chartho Na Kaamo Na Mokshah
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||3||

Na Punyam Na Paapam Na Saukhyam Na Duhkham
Na Mantro Na Teertham Na Vedaa Na Yajnaah.
Aham Bhojanam Naiva Bhojyam Na Bhoktaa
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||4||

Na Mrityurna Shankaa Na Me Jaatibhedah
Pitaa Naiva Me Naiva Maataa Na Janma.
Na Bandhurna Mitram Gururnaiva Shishyah
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||5||

Aham Nirvikalpo Niraakaararoopo
Vibhutvaacha Sarvatra Sarvendriyaanaam.
Na Chaasangatam Naiva Muktirna Meyah
Chidaanandaroopah Shivo'ham Shivo'ham.||6||

|| Iti Shrimachhankaracharyavirachitam Nirvanashatkam Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
(1) मैं मन, बुद्धि, अहंकार या चित्त नहीं हूँ। न कान हूँ, न जिह्वा, न नासिका, न नेत्र। न आकाश, न पृथ्वी, न तेज, न वायु। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ, मैं शिव हूँ।

(2) मैं प्राण नहीं हूँ, न पञ्चवायु (प्राण, अपान, व्यान, उदान, समान)। न सप्तधातु (रस, रक्त, मांस, मेद, अस्थि, मज्जा, शुक्र), न पञ्चकोश (अन्नमय, प्राणमय, मनोमय, विज्ञानमय, आनन्दमय)। न वाक्, न हाथ, न पैर, न उपस्थ, न पायु। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ।

(3) मुझमें न द्वेष है, न राग; न लोभ है, न मोह। न मद है, न मात्सर्य भाव। न मेरा धर्म है, न अर्थ, न काम, न मोक्ष। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ।

(4) न पुण्य, न पाप; न सुख, न दुःख। न मन्त्र, न तीर्थ, न वेद, न यज्ञ। मैं भोजन नहीं, न भोज्य, न भोक्ता। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ।

(5) न मृत्यु, न शंका, न मेरा कोई जाति-भेद। न पिता, न माता, न जन्म। न बन्धु, न मित्र, न गुरु, न शिष्य। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ।

(6) मैं निर्विकल्प हूँ, निराकार स्वरूप हूँ। विभुत्व से सर्वत्र सर्वेन्द्रियों में हूँ। न आसक्ति है, न मुक्ति, न कोई प्रमेय। मैं चिदानन्द रूप शिव हूँ, मैं शिव हूँ।
benefits: The quintessential Advaita Vedanta composition for Self-realization
Establishes the truth 'I am pure consciousness and bliss (Chidananda)'
Frees the mind from attachment to body, senses, and ego
Transcends all dualities - pleasure/pain, virtue/sin, birth/death
Regular recitation leads to deep meditative states
Traditionally recited for removal of ignorance and reveals one's true nature as shiva (auspiciousness)
description: The Nirvana Shatakam, known in Sanskrit as निर्वाण षट्कम् (आत्म षट्कम्), is a sacred prayer in praise of Lord Shiva. This sacred composition is sourced from Adi Shankaracharya and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to systematically negates all false identifications (neti-neti) as well as free the mind from attachment to body, senses, and ego. Additionally, it is known to establish the truth 'I am pure consciousness and bliss (Chidananda)'. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 6 verses, it can be completed in approximately 4 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Nirvana Shatakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Nirvana Shatakam (Atma Shatakam) by Shankaracharya - 'Mano Buddhyahankara' in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Six verses on Self-realization and Advaita.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Nirvana Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Nirvana Shatakam (निर्वाण षट्कम्), also called the Atma Shatakam (आत्म षट्कम्), is a six-verse (shatakam = six) Sanskrit Vedantic hymn composed by Adi Shankaracharya (c. 788-820 CE) — the supreme philosopher-saint of Advaita Vedanta. It is one of the principal compositions of the entire Hindu non-dualist tradition and the most concise summary of the Self-realisation taught in the Upanishads. The hymn is structured as a systematic neti-neti (not this, not this) negation: in each verse Shankara denies that he is identical with one set of bodily / mental / social / cosmic categories, and concludes each verse with the supreme affirmation Chidananda-rupah Shivoham Shivoham (I am of the form of Consciousness-Bliss; I am Shiva, I am Shiva). The six verses progressively negate: (1) mind, intellect, ego, chitta and the five elements; (2) prana, dhatus, koshas and organs; (3) emotions and the four purusharthas; (4) virtue, sin, ritual; (5) family, caste, birth, death; (6) all relativity itself — leaving only pure undifferentiated Atman. (Source: Adi Shankaracharya Nirvana Shatakam.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous story behind the composition of the Nirvana Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: According to the Sankara Digvijaya hagiographies (especially Madhava-Vidyaranya's Sankshepa-Sankara-Vijaya), the young Shankara, having renounced the world at age eight, journeyed from Kerala to the banks of the Narmada in search of a guru. There he met Sri Govindapada, the disciple of Gaudapada, in his cave-ashram. When Govindapada asked the young brahmacharin Who are you? the boy spontaneously composed and recited these six verses as his answer — declaring that he was not the body, not the senses, not the mind, not even the categories of pleasure and pain or birth and death — but Chidananda-rupah Shivoham, pure Consciousness-Bliss, Shiva himself. Govindapada accepted him as his disciple and initiated him into sannyasa. The six verses thus represent the original spontaneous outpouring of Shankara's Atma-jnana and remain the foundational autobiographical statement of the entire Advaita Vedanta tradition. The hymn is recited at every Advaita ashram and Shankara matha across India. (Source: Sankara Digvijaya hagiographies; Advaita parampara tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Mano Buddhyahankara Chittani Naham mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening line of Verse 1 — Mano buddhyahankara chittani naham — declares: I am not the mind (manas), I am not the intellect (buddhi), I am not the ego (ahankara), I am not the citta (storehouse of impressions / unconscious mind). These four — manas, buddhi, ahankara, chitta — together form the antah-karana (inner instrument) of classical Vedanta, the entire psychological apparatus through which the world is experienced. Shankara's first move is to deny identification with the antah-karana itself: I am not even the mind that thinks, the intellect that judges, the ego that says I, or the chitta that remembers. The verse continues: Na cha shrotra-jihve na cha ghrana-netre — I am not the ear, the tongue, the nose, the eyes (the sense-organs); Na cha vyoma bhumir-na tejo na vayuh — I am not space (vyoma), earth (bhumi), fire (tejas) or air (vayu) — that is, none of the five great elements. The entire material-mental-cosmic apparatus is denied. Then: Chidananda-rupah Shivoham Shivoham — I am of the form of pure Consciousness (chit) and Bliss (ananda); I am Shiva (the Auspicious One, the supreme Brahman), I am Shiva. (Source: Nirvana Shatakam verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Chidananda Rupah Shivoham?
seoFaqs.answer: Chidananda-rupah Shivoham Shivoham — repeated as the refrain of every verse — is one of the most famous moksha-mahavakyas in Sanskrit. Word-by-word: Chit = pure undifferentiated consciousness; Ananda = bliss; Rupah = of the form, of the nature; Shivoham = Shiva + aham = I am Shiva. The full meaning: I am of the form of Consciousness-Bliss; I am Shiva, I am Shiva. Why Shiva? In Shankara's Advaita, the word Shiva does not merely refer to the iconic deity Mahadev but is used in its etymological sense — Shiva = the Auspicious One, the absolute Good, the supreme Brahman in whom there is no defect or limitation. The same Brahman is called Brahman from one angle, Shiva from another, Vishnu / Narayana from another. Shankara, born as a Smarta and initiated as a Shaiva, uses Shiva as the most concise name for the absolute. The doubling Shivoham Shivoham is the practice of mahavakya-anusandhana — repeated affirmation until the realisation becomes unshakeable. (Sources: Nirvana Shatakam refrain; Advaita Vedanta mahavakya tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is negated in each of the six verses of the Nirvana Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The six verses progressively strip away every false identification (a systematic neti-neti). Verse 1 negates: manas, buddhi, ahankara, chitta (the antah-karana), the five jnanendriyas (ear, tongue, nose, eyes — and skin implied), and the five mahabhutas (space, earth, fire, air — and water). Verse 2 negates: prana and the pancha-vayus (prana, apana, vyana, udana, samana), the sapta-dhatus (the seven bodily constituents), the pancha-koshas (the five sheaths from annamaya to anandamaya), and the five karmendriyas (vak, pani, pada, upastha, payu). Verse 3 negates: dvesha-raga (aversion-desire), lobha-moha (greed-delusion), mada (intoxication / pride), matsarya (jealousy), and the four purusharthas — dharma, artha, kama, moksha. Verse 4 negates: punya-papa (virtue-sin), saukhya-duhkha (pleasure-pain), mantra, tirtha, vedas, yajnas, and the triad of bhojana / bhojya / bhokta (eating / food / eater). Verse 5 negates: mrityu (death), shanka (doubt), jati-bheda (caste-distinction), pita-mata (father-mother), janma (birth), bandhu-mitra (kinsman-friend), guru-shishya (teacher-disciple). Verse 6 negates: every remaining duality — asanga (attachment), mukti (liberation as object), meyatva (being a measurable object). What remains is the indivisible non-dual Atman. (Source: Nirvana Shatakam verses 1-6.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Nirvana Shatakam be recited and what benefits does it grant?
seoFaqs.answer: The Nirvana Shatakam is recited daily by Advaita Vedanta sannyasis, brahmacharis and serious mumukshus (seekers of liberation) across India and worldwide. Specific occasions include: Monday (Somvar — sacred to Shiva), Maha Shivaratri (the supreme Shiva festival, when the Shivoham refrain is most potent), Guru Purnima (the festival of the spiritual teacher — recited in honour of Adi Shankara and the Advaita parampara), Shankara Jayanti (Vaishakha Shukla Panchami — the birth-anniversary of Adi Shankara), in every Vedanta gurukula and Shankara-matha (Sringeri, Dwarka, Puri, Joshimath, Kanchipuram), and as the principal mantra during deep meditation (dhyana) and contemplation (manana). Tradition attributes these benefits: (1) realisation of the Atman as Brahman (the supreme moksha-fruit); (2) freedom from all false identification with body, mind, ego, social roles; (3) destruction of birth-death cycle (na me jati-bhedah, na janma); (4) transcendence of the dvandvas (pleasure-pain, virtue-sin); (5) the experience of nirvikalpa samadhi; (6) absolute fearlessness (since there is no other to fear). (Sources: Advaita parampara; Sringeri Sharada Peetham daily liturgy.)
